title: Date and walnut scones
subtitle: British scones with chopped dates and walnuts
excerpt: Classic British scones enriched with chopped Medjool dates and walnuts — sweet from the dates, with a slight crunch from the nuts. The method is the same as a plain scone: work the fat in quickly, add the liquid at once, handle the dough minimally. Better than a plain scone with a mug of tea.
sourceNotes: Date and walnut scones are part of the British tea-time and café scone tradition, a natural variation on the plain scone that substitutes dried fruit and nuts for the standard currant or raisin. The combination of dates and walnuts, popular in British baking broadly, appears in British domestic baking literature from the mid-twentieth century. The recipe here synthesises the established British domestic formula for enriched scones.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully baked scones once cool. Thaw at room temperature for 1 hour or warm in a low oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best within 2 hours of baking. Scones stale quickly; freeze rather than storing overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 200°C. High heat is essential for scones — it drives the rise before the outside sets. They are done when the top is deep gold, the sides have opened slightly, and the base sounds hollow when tapped.

glossaryTerms.definition: When cutting scones, push the cutter straight down and pull it straight up — never twist. Twisting seals the cut edge, preventing the scone from rising freely and producing a lopsided bake. Dip the cutter in flour between each cut to prevent sticking. Scones cut with a straight press rise evenly and split cleanly along the sides.

content.text: Good scones depend on cold fat, a hot oven, and minimal handling. The only technique worth pausing on is the
: press straight down and lift straight up, never twist. Twisting seals the edges and the scone will not rise evenly. Everything else is quick and simple.

Heat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the sugar, dates, and walnuts. Beat together the milk and egg, then pour into the dry ingredients and mix with a knife or spatula until the dough just comes together — it will be slightly sticky. Do not overwork.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface and press to 2.5 cm thickness — do not roll. Cut into rounds of 5 to 6 cm diameter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the lined tray. Brush the tops with milk. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until deep gold on top and the sides have opened slightly. The base should sound hollow when tapped. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes before serving.
